canonical_definition: Across the provided literature, Llama Guard 3 is predominantly characterized as an automated tool for evaluating the safety of language model outputs, frequently described as a "safety classifier," "automated judge," or "content safety classification LLM." Its primary documented use is to measure `Harmful content generation` by classifying model responses as "harmful or safe." Papers specify that it is capable of identifying various types of harmful content, such as "violence, self-harm, illegal activities, and inappropriate sexual content." It is operationalized as a "discriminator in attack evaluation" and as an "auxiliary detection tool," with one source noting the "close alignment of Llama-guard-3 and human evaluations" ("Measuring memorization in language models via probabilistic extraction"). While most sources focus on this safety classification role, a few papers describe more specific or distinct applications. For example, one study employs it to "evaluate the fairness of model outputs," and another uses it to assess safety by measuring a model's "refusal rate to harmful queries." A different framing appears in one paper that characterizes Llama Guard 3 not just as a post-hoc evaluation instrument but as an active "neural defense model" designed to "detect and block jailbreak attempts and unsafe content."
papers: FineReason: Evaluating and ImprovingLLMs’ Deliberate Reasoning through Reflective Puzzle Solving
M³GQA: A Multi-Entity Multi-Hop Multi-Setting Graph Question Answering Benchmark
Breach By A Thousand Leaks: Unsafe Information Leakage in 'Safe' AI Responses
FairMT-Bench: Benchmarking Fairness for Multi-turn Dialogue in Conversational LLMs
Maximizing the Potential of Synthetic Data: Insights from Random Matrix Theory
Investigating the (De)Composition Capabilities of Large Language Models in Natural-to-Formal Language Conversion
Measuring memorization in language models via probabilistic extraction

definitions.text: An open-source content safety classification LLM used as an auxiliary detection tool to evaluate the fairness of model outputs.
definitions.paper: FairMT-Bench: Benchmarking Fairness for Multi-turn Dialogue in Conversational LLMs

definitions.text: Safety classifier used to judge whether model responses are safe in the alignment experiment.
definitions.paper: Maximizing the Potential of Synthetic Data: Insights from Random Matrix Theory

definitions.text: A semantic classifier capable of identifying various types of harmful content, including violence, self-harm, illegal activities, and inappropriate sexual content, used as a discriminator in attack evaluation.
definitions.paper: Investigating the (De)Composition Capabilities of Large Language Models in Natural-to-Formal Language Conversion

definitions.text: Automated content moderation system used as a judge to classify model-generated responses as harmful or safe based on predefined safety policies.
definitions.paper: Measuring memorization in language models via probabilistic extraction

definitions.text: A specialized language model used as an evaluation tool to assess the safety of other models' outputs by measuring their refusal rate to harmful queries.
definitions.paper: M³GQA: A Multi-Entity Multi-Hop Multi-Setting Graph Question Answering Benchmark

relationships.paper: Investigating the (De)Composition Capabilities of Large Language Models in Natural-to-Formal Language Conversion
relationships.evidence: Then evaluate the harmfulness score on its responses with the Llama-Guard-3-8B (Llama Team, 2024). We use 70% harmful prompts in the AdvBench dataset (Zou et al., 2023b) for InferAligner, Vaccine, and our SaLoRA. (Section 5.1).

naacl25: Investigating the (De)Composition Capabilities of Large Language Models in Natural-to-Formal Language Conversion
